events.title: Drought in Tanzania
events.body:
Between September 2021 and January 2022, prolonged drought killed at least 306,358 cattle, goats, sheep, and donkeys across Tanzania. In Simanjiro district alone, more than 92,000 animals were lost, stripping many Maasai families of their primary source of food and income.

In response, Maasai women in the Monduli and Longido districts have organized fodder farms across about 75 hectares of community land, growing drought-resistant grasses that stay green longer than natural pasture. Around 250 women directly manage these farms, while thousands of herding families buy the feed during dry seasons.

The effort, coordinated by the Pastoral Women's Council, now includes 10 grass seed banks across eight villages. In 2025, one seed bank alone earned roughly $2,500 from seed sales and sold over 1,100 hay bales. What began as a way to keep animals alive through dry spells has grown into a steady source of income for many households across the region.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Maasai pastoral communities in northern Tanzania where drought has destroyed livestock and livelihoods
events.alert_summary: Prolonged drought killed at least 306,358 livestock across Tanzania between September 2021 and January 2022.

events.title: Other in China
events.body:
A fire at the Wang Fuk housing complex in Hong Kong last November killed 168 people — the deadliest the city had seen in over 70 years. The blaze also left thousands of residents without homes. Authorities have now laid 25 charges against two companies and seven individuals, including manslaughter, conspiracy to defraud, and corruption.

An independent investigation found that fire alarms in seven of eight residential blocks had been deactivated, and windows had been boarded up with flammable foam panels during ongoing renovation work. Investigators concluded that nearly all fire safety systems failed on the day of the fire due to human factors. Workers were also reported to have discarded lit cigarette butts across the site.

Thirty-five people have been arrested on manslaughter and fraud allegations. A separate anti-corruption body arrested 23 others, including consultants and contractors. The case has been adjourned to September for further investigation.

events.title: Disease in Congo 05/15/2025
events.body:
The Democratic Republic of the Congo is battling its 17th Ebola outbreak, with 550 confirmed cases and 101 deaths reported so far. The outbreak, caused by the Bundibugyo strain of Ebola — a rarer form for which no approved vaccine or treatment exists — was announced on May 15 but went undetected for weeks before that, leaving health workers struggling to catch up. Cases have spread across 25 health zones in three provinces: Ituri, North Kivu, and South Kivu.

The response has been severely hampered by armed groups in Ituri, limiting access to affected communities. A burial team was recently attacked at a cemetery in Bunia, seriously injuring two people. Mistrust among local populations has added further difficulty.

The outbreak has also reached neighboring Uganda, which has reported 19 cases and two deaths. Travel bans and border closures have been introduced by several countries, though health officials have urged authorities to reconsider blanket restrictions as ineffective.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera

The storm is tracking toward Central America, with Honduras, El Salvador, Nicaragua, and Guatemala all identified as countries in its path. All four nations are considered highly vulnerable to tropical weather events.

No population has been reported as directly affected by damaging wind speeds at this time, though conditions can change quickly as a tropical storm moves over land. Residents in low-lying and coastal areas of these countries are encouraged to monitor local warnings closely.

events.title: Disease in Congo
events.body:
An Ebola outbreak centered in three provinces of eastern DR Congo has now reached 380 confirmed cases and 60 deaths inside the country, with 15 additional confirmed cases and one death in neighboring Uganda. Earlier counts suggested more than 1,000 suspected cases, but laboratories have since ruled out many patients who had fever from other causes such as malaria. The drop in numbers reflects better testing, not a less dangerous situation.

Health teams face serious obstacles. Only about 45% of people who came into contact with Ebola patients are being traced and monitored, far short of the 90% needed to control an outbreak. The affected area is largely rural, remote, and troubled by armed conflict. This week, a burial team was reportedly attacked in South Kivu province and forced to leave a body behind — raising fears of further spread.

Health officials warn that without stronger action, this outbreak — caused by a rare strain with no available vaccine — could grow as large as the devastating 2014–2016 West Africa crisis. Rebuilding community trust is seen as essential to stopping the disease.
events.prayer: Communities in eastern DR Congo and Uganda amid a dangerous Ebola outbreak with limited contact tracing

events.title: Disease in Congo
events.body:
An Ebola outbreak is spreading in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, where health workers face not only the disease itself but also deep fear and hostility from local communities. In one village, health workers who arrived to safely bury a person who had died from Ebola were threatened and forced to leave, with residents warning that armed rebels would be called if they stayed.

Safe burials are critical to stopping Ebola from spreading, since the virus can be transmitted through contact with the body of someone who has died from the disease. When health teams are turned away, the risk of further infections rises sharply.

The situation reflects a wider challenge in the region, where years of conflict and distrust make it difficult to reach people who need help and contain a disease that is both deadly and frightening.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Communities in eastern DR Congo caught between Ebola and fear of health workers

events.title: Disease in United States 05/23/2025
events.body:
A flesh-eating parasite known as the New World Screwworm has been found in cattle in southern Texas for the first time in 60 years. Two infected calves have been identified near the town of La Pryor, close to the Mexican border. The parasite — a fly whose larvae burrow through the living flesh of warm-blooded animals — can kill an animal if left untreated. Texas has declared a state of disaster, warning the outbreak poses an imminent threat to its agricultural industry.

Officials have set up a control zone around the affected area and put in place quarantines and movement restrictions. Canada has responded by banning livestock from Texas that may have recently crossed the border.

To slow the spread, authorities plan to release hundreds of millions of sterile flies and deploy detection dogs. The outbreak is part of a wider spread moving northward through Central America and Mexico. Experts warn the parasite is likely to continue spreading through the summer months.
events.prayer: Farmers and livestock across Texas facing the spread of flesh-eating screwworm for the first time in decades
